text: A forty seven years old gentleman with obstructive jaundice with bilirubin of fourteen with CA ninety nine of four hundred. On evaluation there was a four into three centimeter peri-ampullary growth with double duct sign without vascular invasion and the lymph node involvement.
The surgical setup includes a 10 mm supraumbilical port and a 15 mm port designated for staplers, along with additional working and retraction ports visible in the abdominal area.

After entering the abdomen, the surgeon mobilizes the right colon by entering the lesser sac, taking down the hepatic flexure, and separating congenital adhesions between the retroperitoneum, right colon, and duodenum. The procedure progresses towards the inferior vena cava for extended cocreation.

The surgeon mobilizes congenital adhesions between the second and third parts of the duodenum (D-II, D-III). The gallbladder is retracted upwards to facilitate this process. The C-loop of the duodenum is completely mobilized, revealing the left border of the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the left renal vein.
After completing the extended caucarization, the surgeon returns to the lesser sac to divide the gastrocolic trunk of the loop of Henle.

The surgeon carefully dissects the adhesions between the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and the uncinate process, progressing towards the portal vein to create a surgical passage known as the 'tunnel of love.' The dissection involves meticulous separation of small vessels above the SMV, ensuring clear visualization of the tunnel from both above and below.

The surgeon dissects adhesions between the stomach and the upper border of the pancreas, proceeding towards the superior border of the pancreas. The hepatic artery lymph node is dissected to remove a gauze piece from above. The gauze piece is located at the superior border of the pancreas, just above the SMV and portal vein.
The surgeon uses a guide to prevent torrential bleeding from the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) during the dissection.

The surgeon begins the stomach transection as part of the pylorus-resecting pancreaticoduodenectomy, aiming to preserve as much of the stomach as possible. The portal lymph nodal dissection is initiated, starting from below upwards, with the hepatic artery lymph node being dissected first, followed by the portal vein lymph nodes, progressing towards the hilum. The lymph nodes are visible in the surgeon's left hand.

The hepatic artery is clearly visible, surrounded by lymph nodes. The surgeon begins skeletonizing the gastroduodenal artery (GDA), a critical step in pancreaticoduodenectomy due to its propensity to bleed. Metal clips are applied to the GDA as markers before cutting.
The surgeon skeletonizes the common bile duct, loops it with umbilical tape, and sets it aside, ensuring clear visualization and control of the structure.

Stay sutures are placed at the upper border of the pancreas for traction. The surgeon then transects the pancreas at the lower border using a harmonic scalpel. As the dissection approaches the pancreatic duct, scissors are used with caution. The pancreatic duct, approximately seven millimeters wide, is clearly visible. The gauze piece is seen over the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein.

The surgeon performs infracolic dissection following the jejunal transection using an endo GIA purple-sixty stapler. The dissection progresses towards the duodenojejunal junction (DJJ), focusing on the first jejunal vein and the inferior mesenteric vein. The infracolic jejunum is mobilized and pulled into the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon holds the entire resected specimen in the left hand, highlighting the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) located just behind the superior mesenteric vein (SMV). Careful dissection is performed around the SMA after sealing the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels with a Ligasure device.

The surgeon carefully divides tissue using a ligature device, focusing on the area near the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). As the dissection progresses towards the upper border, the superior pancreaticoduodenal vessels become visible.
The surgeon mobilizes the gallbladder and transects the bile duct, then sets the specimen aside and performs a thorough wash of the surgical area.

The video shows the anatomy with the transected pancreas, pancreatic duct (PD), superior mesenteric vein (SMV), portal vein, gastroduodenal artery (GDA), and bile duct. The surgeon creates a mesocolic rent and pulls the jejunum up towards the pancreas. The pancreaticojejunostomy is initiated using three to four binding stitches with 2-0 Vicryl, threading through the pancreas and applying seromuscular stitches over the jejunum.

The surgeon carefully performs an entrotomy on the jejunum, creating a small opening. A duct-to-mucosa pancreatic jejunostomy is initiated using interrupted 4-0 PDS sutures. The first stitch is placed at the nine o'clock position, followed by posterior stitches, then at three o'clock. The wide duct pancreatic ductal and jejunal mucosal anastomosis is constructed with approximately eight to nine stitches, including anterior stitches. Binding stitches are tied to seal the pancreatic surface.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy using a continuous suture technique with four-row PDS. The anastomosis begins at the left-sided corner and progresses towards the right. For smaller ducts, interrupted sutures are used posteriorly, followed by continuous sutures anteriorly. For larger ducts, continuous sutures are applied both posteriorly and anteriorly to prevent the bowstring effect.

The surgeon begins the end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy from the left side, tying sutures at the right corner. Next, the end-to-side gastrojejunostomy is performed, with a gastric enterotomy made over the bougie. The jejunal enterotomy is created below the previous staple line using an endo GA stapler with a purple cartridge. The gastrojejunostomy enterotomy is closed with continuous 3-0 PDS sutures, starting from the posterior and moving to the corner, then completing the anastomosis at the right corner with an anterior stitch. The mucosa-to-mucosa inverting anastomosis is maintained throughout the procedure.

The surgeon ties the final stitch of the gastrojejunostomy, completing the pancreaticojejunostomy and hepaticojejunostomy. The specimen is extracted through a small umbilical incision, and a feeding jejunostomy is performed through the drain placed in Morrison's pouch.
